title: How to choose wool for felting
subtitle: What micron count, staple length, and breed mean for wet and needle felting
excerpt: Wool felts because barbed fibre scales open and lock under heat and agitation. Different breeds produce different fibre diameters and scale structures, which directly determine how fast and how firmly a given roving will felt. This guide explains what to look for when choosing roving for a first project.
sourceNotes: The fibre science underlying felting (scale structure, micron count, cortical cells) is documented in the technical textile literature. This article draws on the physical properties of wool fibres as observed across the different breed classifications used in the British wool trade.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measure of fibre diameter. One micron is one millionth of a metre. Finer fibres (lower micron count) felt faster and produce a softer fabric; coarser fibres (higher micron count) felt more slowly and produce a harder-wearing fabric.

The length of an individual fibre lock as it comes from the fleece, before processing into roving. Short staples (under 8 cm) felt readily; long staples (over 15 cm) felt slowly and are better suited to spinning than to felting.

Wool fibres are coated in tiny overlapping scales, like roof tiles. Under heat and agitation, these scales open and catch on neighbouring fibres, creating the irreversible entanglement that makes felt.

content.text: The wool roving you choose determines how quickly your project felts, how soft the finished fabric feels, and how much the piece shrinks during the process. The key variables are
micron count
(fibre diameter),
staple length
, and the breed's
scale structure
. Understanding these three things removes most of the guesswork from a new project.
Micron count: the main variable

Wool fibres range from around 15 microns (very fine Merino) to over 40 microns (coarse carpet wool). For felting, the practical range runs from 15 to 28 microns. Finer fibres (under 22 microns) felt quickly, produce a soft surface, and work at lower temperatures. Coarser fibres (24-28 microns) felt more slowly, produce a harder surface with more texture, and need more heat and agitation. Above 28 microns the felt can be brittle.

The right micron count depends on the project. For a baby bootie or a fine needle-felted figure that will touch skin, stay below 22 microns. For a floor mat or a rug patch, 24-28 microns produces a harder-wearing surface that is worth the extra working time.
Breed guide for common felting rovings
Merino (15-22 microns) is the universal beginner fibre. It felts fast, felts soft, takes dye well, and is available in every craft shop. Start here if this is your first felting project.

Corriedale (24-27 microns) is the next step. It felts noticeably more slowly than Merino and produces a harder surface. The staple is longer and the fibre is more forgiving to handle. Many felters prefer Corriedale for large panels and three-dimensional projects where Merino sometimes over-felts before the piece reaches the right size.

Shetland (23-28 microns) is a fine-to-medium British fibre with good scale structure. Felts well and has a characteristic soft-but-resilient handle. Strong traditional connection to the British felting and spinning traditions.

Bluefaced Leicester (BFL) (24-28 microns, long staple) felts more slowly than Merino and has a distinctive lustre that shows through the felt surface. Not ideal for needle felting because the long staple resists the short stab; better suited to wet felting for accessories where the lustre adds value.

Romney (36-40 microns) is a long-stapled medium-coarse British fibre. Used for rugs and floor mats where durability matters more than softness. Takes longer to felt and produces a harder, more textured surface. Not suitable for anything worn next to skin.
Fibres that don't felt

Superwash-treated wool does not felt. The superwash process removes or coats the fibre scales so the wool survives machine washing without shrinking, which is exactly the property that makes felting possible. Any bag of roving or yarn labelled superwash or machine-washable will not felt under any amount of heat and agitation. Check the label before buying roving for a felting project.

Synthetic fibres (acrylic, nylon, polyester) do not felt. Silk and plant fibres (cotton, linen) do not felt either, though they can be used as a base fabric in nuno felting with wool on top. The felting is done by the wool, not the base fabric.
Pre-felt batts versus roving

Pre-felt batt is roving that has been lightly felted into a flat, stable sheet that can be cut into shapes. It is useful for nuno felting (cuts cleanly, holds its shape during placement) and for pictorial needle-felted work (cut shapes can be placed precisely on a surface before needling). Raw roving is used for building up base shapes and laying cross-hatched layers in wet felting. The two products come from the same fibre at different stages of preparation.
